definition: A *potion* is a prepared draught meant to be drunk for magical or medicinal effect — a love-philter, a sleeping-drink, a cure, a poison. Where a *tincture* is an extracted essence and a talisman is worn, a potion is swallowed, its power entering the body directly. The category slides easily from healing to harm: Circe's cup and the physician's cordial are, in form, the same object.
history: Latin *potio*, 'a drink' (from *potare*, 'to drink'), is the plain root — and its twin. The very same word, traveling a different French road, becomes 'poison,' so that potion and poison are perfect etymological doubles, and the sources honor the pairing: the Greek magical papyri promise one who is unaffected by potions and poison. The medical and the maleficent never fully part. Renaissance recipe books record purgative 'potions,' while witchcraft trials turn on the 'love potion,' the *pharmaka* that heal, madden, or kill by the same draught.

def_sources.label: Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England: John Dee

commentary: The love-potion opens onto one of the liveliest areas of ancient-magic scholarship. Christopher Faraone's *Ancient Greek Love Magic* argued that erotic magic split by gender and aim: violent *agōgē* spells to seize and compel, used mostly by men, against gentler *philia* potions to keep affection, associated with women — and that the sources' panic over women poisoning men (*pharmaka* meaning both potion and poison) encodes real anxieties about female agency. The debate turns the love-potion from a quaint charm into evidence about gender, power, and who was permitted to desire.

commentary_sources.label: Christopher A. Faraone, Ancient Greek Love Magic
Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation

etymology.note: 'Poison' is the very same Latin word arriving by a different French road — a potion and a poison are perfect etymological twins.
etymology.hook: Potion and poison: one Latin word, two fates.

flavor_snippets.html: "Did nephew spit up again? Oh, good! Auntie needed to make a new batch of <mark>potions</mark> for the merrow market."

"Where our <mark>potions</mark> and powders fail, perhaps nature will succeed."
—Reyhan, Samite Alchemist

"Powders and <mark>potions</mark> are nothing compared to faith."
—Baris, Serra Inquisitor
Their acorn hunt was unsuccessful, but they did find the wizard's experimental <mark>potion</mark> cache.
"No need for a hurda? How about these maps to recently discovered tombs? Or these vials of invisible <mark>potion</mark>? Very fancy . . ."
"I created that <mark>potion</mark> to rob me of my spider-powers. I wanted it to change me forever. But not like this! NOT LIKE THIS!"
It took years to finesse his <mark>potion</mark> of necroskitter slime and bogslither teeth. Finally, *he* would prank the fae.
Oculomancers see the ideal <mark>potion</mark> ingredient. The bats don't see it that way.
"How about a three-piece combo of Hi-<mark>Potion</mark>, Ether, and Phoenix Pinion for 444 Gil?"
The parasitic moss that grows in the folds of the goobbue's back is highly valued as an ingredient in various <mark>potions</mark> and medicines.
Treated with a powerful mix of herbs and <mark>potions</mark>, its wrappings have a remarkable preservative effect, even for the living.
